data.title: Alaska Air Group Reports Higher 1Q Fuel Costs, Expects Spike in 2Q — OPIS
data.description:
Alaska Air Group reported higher fuel costs for the first quarter of the year and expects fuel costs to jump significantly in the coming months, according to financial filings released Monday evening.

For the first quarter, Alaska reported spending $796 million on fuel and related costs, a 17% spike from the first three months of 2025.

The air group also reported the average fuel price per gallon was $2.98/gal for the first three months of the year, a 14.2% jump from the same quarter a year ago.

While costs increased, Alaska also reported using 1.9% more fuel in the quarter, some 267 million gallons.

Alaska Air Group revealed in the filing that they source about 55% of their jet fuel from the West Coast, about 20% from Singapore and about 25% from other regions, including the U.S. Gulf Coast.

Going into the second quarter of the year, Alaska cited fuel costs as the largest source of near-term uncertainty," and predicts fuel costs in April to average $4.75/gal and to average about $4.50/gal for the quarter.

"This assumption adds approximately $600 million of expense to the second quarter," according to the filing. "We [Alaska Air Group] expect to consume approximately 297 million gallons of fuel in the quarter based on our current capacity plan," said the filing.

Looking ahead, Alaska has suspended financial outlooks for the full year, citing increasing fuel costs and price volatility.

"For full year 2026, our visibility to earnings is limited due primarily to ongoing fuel price volatility. Until conditions stabilize and we have better sight to earnings beyond the current quarter, we have suspended full-year guidance. Similarly, for the second quarter, the range of potential financial outcomes remains wide and difficult to predict, as recent geopolitical factors have resulted in sharp and unpredictable changes in fuel prices," the air group said in the financial filing.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Cory Wilchek, cwilchek@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com

data.title: Banco Macro S.A. Informs the Market of the Filing of its Annual Report on form 20-F for the Fiscal Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
BUENOS AIRES, Argentina, April 21, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — Banco Macro S.A. (; ByMA: "BMA") ("Banco Macro") announces today the filing of its annual report on Form 20-F and its annual audited financial statements for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025 (the "2025 Annual Report") with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC"). The 2025 Annual Report can be accessed by visiting either the SEC's website at www.sec.gov in the Section "Search for Company Filings" under CIK code No. 0001347426 or Banco Macro's Investor Relations website at www.macro.com.ar/relaciones-inversores under the Financial Information/Financial Information & Reports /Sec Filing link. In addition, shareholders may receive a hard copy of Banco Macro's complete annual audited financial statements as of and for the year ended December 31, 2025 free of charge within a reasonable period of time by making a request through Banco Macro's Investor Relations website (www.macro.com.ar/relaciones-inversores), writing to investorelations@macro.com.ar or contacting Banco Marco's Investor Relations Department at (5411) 5222 6682.

This press release includes statements concerning potential future events involving Banco Macro that could differ materially from the events that actually occur. The differences could be caused by a number of risks, uncertainties and factors relating to Banco Macro's business. Banco Macro will not update any forward-looking statements made in this press release to reflect future events or developments.

IR Contact in Buenos Aires:

Jorge Scarinci | Chief Financial Officer

Nicolás A. Torres | Investor Relations

E-mail: investorelations@macro.com.ar | Phone: (54 11) 5222 6682

data.title: Goldman Sachs maintains 2026 copper price, surplus forecasts
data.description:
Goldman Sachs on Tuesday maintained its forecast for the copper price to average $12,650 per metric ton this year and its estimate of a 490,000-ton 2026 surplus for the metal.

However, the bank flagged risks to copper supply from potential sulphuric acid shortages should disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz continue.

The bank said the disruption, combined with China's decision to ban sulphuric acid exports from May 1, could tighten a market critical for copper production.

Sulphur and sulphuric acid are key inputs for solvent extraction and electrowinning, a process that accounts for 17% of global copper supply.

Goldman said the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Chile were the most exposed to disruptions in sulphur flows.

The U.S.-Israel war on Iran has hit the supply of energy goods and other materials, as Iran has effectively blocked the key Strait of Hormuz shipping artery.

President Donald Trump said on Tuesday he did not want to extend the current ceasefire and the U.S. military was "raring to go" if negotiations were not successful.

Companies in the DRC still hold two to three months of inventory, but if supply-chain delays extend beyond late May through June, Goldman estimates the country could curtail about 125,000 tons of production in 2026.

That curtailment would be offset by 140,000 tons of lower copper demand from weaker global growth in the bank's adverse scenario.

Separately, China's ban on sulphuric acid exports lasting through the year would put 200,000 tons of Chilean production at risk, equivalent to 1% of global supply, as the country sourced roughly a third of its acid from China in 2025, the bank added.

data.title: UnitedHealth Beats Expectations, Raises Outlook
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH, Financials) made $7.23 per share in non-GAAP earnings in the first quarter, which was $0.63 more than expected. Revenue grew to $111.72 billion, which was 2% more than the same time last year and $2.06 billion more than expected.

The company's full-year 2026 earnings forecast is now more than $17.35 per share, with adjusted earnings expected to be more than $18.25, which is higher than the consensus expectation of $17.87.

The medical cost ratio for UnitedHealth went down 90 basis points from a year ago to 83.9%, showing that they are better at managing costs. The operating cost ratio, on the other hand, went up to 13.8% since the company kept spending money on technology, like AI, and making its operations better.

The UnitedHealthcare part of the corporation served 49.1 million customers, and operating margins grew to 6.6%. At the same time, the Optum segment made $63.7 billion in sales and $3.3 billion in profits, thanks to development in its healthcare services platform.

Shares went up approximately 2.7% in premarket trading after the data came out, which shows that investors were happy with them.

The results show that UnitedHealth can keep costs down while making smart investments. In the next few quarters, investors should keep an eye on margin developments and how well growth plans are being carried out.

data.title: Fed nominee Warsh says Trump never asked him to pursue any policy path
data.description:
By Michael S. Derby

Federal Reserve Chair nominee Kevin Warsh said President Donald Trump has never asked him to pursue a given path of monetary policy.

"The President never once asked me to commit to any particular interest rate decision, period, and nor would I ever agree to do so if he had, but he never did," Warsh told a Senate panel holding a hearing about his nomination to be Fed Chair. President Donald Trump has repeatedly demanded aggressive rate cuts from the central bank.

data.title: ICE Canola Supported by Comparable Oils
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange continued to rally in the middle of Tuesday trading, getting support from crude and vegetable oils.

Crude oil prices showed modest gains as vessel traffic is still restricted through the Strait of Hormuz. However, there are reports that the U.S. and Iran will meet for a second round of peace talks in Pakistan later this week.

Chicago soyoil surpassed 71 U.S. cents per pound and canola is reacting in kind, said an analyst, noting that July canola's next target is C$740 per metric ton, which hasn't been seen since April 7. European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were also higher.

About 36,400 canola contracts have traded at 10:08 a.m. CDT.

Prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton:

 
           Price      Change 
May       723.90    up 10.00 
Jul       737.90    up 10.80 
Nov       733.10     up 9.60 
Jan       740.50     up 9.70

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: Tractor Supply Says Pet Supply Demand Falls as Shoppers Prioritize Value— Update
data.description:
By Rob Curran

Tractor Supply said customers are spending more cautiously on pet supplies, putting pressure on its companion-animal category.

Chief Executive Harry Lawton III said the company is taking clear and decisive action to address struggles in its pet segment, which includes expansion and investments in products.

The company said category shifts and an unfavorable product mix hurt its pet business.

Shares of the farm-and-ranch retailer were recently down 8.4% at $41.07 in Tuesday trading. The company's stock hit a 52-week low of $40.33, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. The stock was at the lowest intraday level since November 2023, when it hit $39.58.

Gross margin was flat in the latest period, hurt by higher tariffs and delivery-related transportation costs, Tractor Supply said.

Comparable average ticket increased 1.6%, partially offset by a comparable average transaction count decrease of 1%. The company said customers' focus on value and spending priorities led to reduced shopping frequency and trip consolidation.

Selling, general and administrative expenses rose 6.1% to $1.07 billion from $1.01 billion in the year-earlier quarter.

Tractor Supply posted earnings of $164.5 million, or 31 cents a share, down from $179.4 million, or 34 cents a share, a year earlier, and short of the average analyst target of 35 cents a share, as tallied by FactSet.

Revenue rose 3.6% to $3.59 billion, shy of the mean Wall Street target of $3.63 billion, as tallied by FactSet. Same-store sales, or sales in stores and digital channels open a year or more, inched up 0.5%, shy of the average analyst estimate of 1.6%.

For the full year, the Brentwood, Tenn., retailer continues to anticipate earnings in a range between $2.13 and $2.23 a share. Tractor Supply also reiterated its sales projection for growth of 4% to 6% from the 2025 level of $15.52 billion. Same-store sales are seen up 1% to 3%.

Wall Street expects per-share earnings of $2.18, revenue of $16.32 billion, and same-store sales growth of 1.9%.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Carney Taps Canada CEOs for Advice on U.S.-Canada Relations as Crucial Trade Talks Loom — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Prime Minister Mark Carney has tapped chief executives from some of the Canada's biggest companies to advise federal officials on its relationship with the U.S., which the Canadian leader has declared to be a source of economic weakness.

Their appointment to an advisory panel comes as the future of a North American trade treaty that has--with some notable exceptions--largely shielded Canadian exports from President Trump's hefty tariffs is in doubt as Washington begins a formal review this summer. Trump administration officials say the treaty, known as USMCA, requires significant changes, and have mused about breaking up the pact into two bilateral deals.

There also remain tensions between Washington and Ottawa over trade. Analysts warn that the lack of meaningful progress in U.S.-Canada talks to date does not bode well for the future of the $700 billion bilateral trade relationship.

Carney said Tuesday the new advisory committee will provide expertise on U.S.-Canada relations, weeks before Washington begins a formal review USMCA. Trump administration officials say preliminary talks with Canada have proven challenging, whereas negotiations with Mexico have proven fruitful and are moving to an advanced stage.

Among the Canadian CEOs Carney has named to the 24-member panel are Darryl White of Bank of Montreal; Tracy Robinson of Canadian National Railway; Ken Seitz of Nutrien; François Poirier of TC Energy; and Jonathan Price of Teck Resources.

Former Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also established an advisory council, although Carney's version is notable for the inclusion of blue-chip CEOs whose companies rely on sales to the U.S. to drive earnings.

The committee is scheduled to hold its first meeting on April 27, Carney said.

Over the weekend, Carney released a 10-minute statement on YouTube that focused on his government's plan to restructure Canada's economic and security landscape to address the threats posed by the Trump administration. "Many of our former strengths, based on our close ties to America, have become our weaknesses, weaknesses that we must correct," Carney said.

Carney said it's crucial that Canada strike deeper trading ties with other parts of the world. "We can't control the disruption coming from our neighbors. We bet our future on the hope that it will suddenly stop. But we can control what happens here," Carney said in a video titled "Forward Guidance."

Eric Miller, head of the Washington-based trade consultancy Rideau Potomac Strategy Group, said Carney's video statement marks a warning from the Liberal government that USMCA renewal talks might fail. "The relationship is headed very much on a wrong track, and we could end up with a very bad outcome from an economic cooperation perspective." He added that Canadian officials unwilling to agree to terms similar to what other jurisdictions, like the European Union and Japan, had initially signed onto, whereby the U.S. slaps tariffs on imports while levies on American goods are either lifted or eased.

"Ottawa's view is that it has a free-trade agreement, and integrated continental economy where the trade dynamics are vastly different," Miller said.

Carney last week secured a majority of the seats in the federal legislature, through persuading opposition-party lawmakers to defect and victories in special elections to fill vacancies. This gives Carney, a former chief central banker, a freer hand to implement his policy agenda, which is focused on accelerating the construction of resource projects and new trade corridors to help sell an increasing share of goods and services to non-U.S. markets.

U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick last week criticized Canada's trade strategy ahead of the USMCA renegotiation, including Ottawa's agreement with China to relax tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles. "They suck," Lutnick said of Canada, in reference to how Canada extracts benefits from America's $30 trillion economy without reciprocal benefits to the U.S.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said Mexico is keen to cut a deal with the U.S. prior to USMCA talks beginning in earnest on tariffs for steel, aluminum and automobiles. U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, who was in Mexico City on Monday, said talks with Mexico have moved to an advanced stage, with the first official bilateral negotiating round on USMCA to begin in late May.

Greer earlier this month said the U.S. "have some issues" with Canada "that haven't been resolved yet." Canada's minister in charge of trade with the U.S., Dominic LeBlanc, said Ottawa and Washington have had conversations and he's optimistic the talks will succeed.

Polling conducted earlier this year in Canada by the Vancouver-based Angus Reid Institute suggested that only 21% of Canadians hold a favorable view of the U.S., and nearly 70% prefer that Carney and federal officials treat the U.S. as a potential threat rather than as an ally.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Tim Cook's secret weapon that turned Apple into a $4 trillion Apple empire
data.description:
By Christine Ji

Tim Cook will leave the CEO post after 15 years marked by supply-chain dominance

Tim Cook will be stepping down from Apple's CEO post in September.

Tim Cook may not have had the same flash as Steve Jobs, but he wielded an unassuming superpower that helped grow Apple into a $4 trillion juggernaut.

Cook first joined Apple (AAPL) in 1998 and assumed the CEO role in August 2011. Before becoming CEO, Cook had established a reputation for himself as a shrewd master of Apple's supply chain. One of his first moves at the company was pioneering a just-in-time inventory model that helped turn a struggling Apple around by aligning the company's raw-material orders with production schedules, slashing Apple's inventory on hand.

Cook's learnings from his time as Apple's chief operating officer helped him expand the company's services business and geographic footprint once he assumed the CEO position. He also drove the company's transition to custom silicon, allowing Apple to have more control over its hardware and software ecosystem through the use of in-house chips.

Cook's expertise in logistics helped the company navigate major supply-chain disruptions, such as the pandemic and various iterations of tariffs. Cook moved swiftly to mitigate the impact of pandemic shutdowns by diversifying Apple's supply chain and shifting production of iPhones from China to India. In doing so, he struck a delicate balance, as he had to shift production in a way that didn't alienate Chinese consumers or cede market share to domestic Chinese competitors.

Handling volatile tariffs required cultivating a savvy business relationship with the man behind them, President Donald Trump. Last August, Apple announced a $2.5 billion commitment to produce glass for its products in Corning's (GLW) Kentucky facility, signaling Apple's intent to invest in American manufacturing. Cook also gifted Trump a custom Apple plaque with a 24-karat gold base. For Cook's efforts, Apple was rewarded a tariff exemption on imported semiconductors and chips.

"Tim Cook had an AMAZING career, almost incomparable, and will go on and continue to do great work for Apple, and whatever else he chooses to work on," Trump wrote on Truth Social. "Quite simply, Tim Cook is an incredible guy!!!"

Now Cook's supply-chain prowess seems to be helping Apple as the consumer-electronics industry faces a severe memory shortage. Apple is reportedly buying large quantities of mobile dynamic random-access memory to squeeze competitors. Meanwhile, Apple isn't letting the memory crunch stand in the way of its objectives. Even in the face of soaring component costs, the company launched its budget laptop, the MacBook Neo, earlier this year.

The MacBook Neo is a prime example of Cook's legacy, Max Weinbach, a technology analyst at Creative Strategies, told MarketWatch. While Apple's vice president of hardware engineering, John Ternus, was the hardware brains behind the product, developing a budget laptop required the company to create its own supply chain and processes. Apple utilized resourceful strategies such as repurposing iPhone 16 chips.

"A lot of people call him a supply-chain guy and Ternus the product guy, and while that's true, the products couldn't exist without the supply chain," Weinbach said of Cook.

Jobs "created the special products, while Tim Cook made sure that the company could make so many of them that everyone could buy one and that Apple would make very large profits while doing so," Richard Windsor, founder of the research firm Radio Free Mobile, wrote in a Tuesday note. "He has been incredibly effective and was the right leader at the right time."

Through Monday's close, Apple shares returned more than 2,300% under Cook's tenure, beating out the S&P 500 index's SPX nearly 700% total return over the same period. Cook grew Apple's appeal to investors, including Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), which made the surprising move to invest in Apple back in 2016 after famously avoiding flashy technology plays.

Read: Apple CEO Tim Cook to step down after overseeing 1,900% stock surge. His successor faces big challenges.

Cook's successes have been reflected in Apple's financials. The latest iPhone 17 line, especially the Air, has been a smash hit in China. Revenue from China rose 38% in the December quarter relative to a year before, fueling Apple's best quarter of iPhone sales ever.

Thanks to Cook's efforts, Ternus is "a company in very good condition," according to Windsor. Cook will step down on Sept. 1, at which point Ternus will take the helm.

-Christine Ji

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
